refined_fr.refined_fr: The NGHE solution shall achieve and maintain operational availability at or above the customer-defined target threshold over the agreed evaluation period.
refined_fr.rationale: The original FR conflates two distinct obligations: (1) meeting a performance target and (2) sustaining it over time. The refined version separates these concerns while remaining solution-neutral. It avoids prescribing how availability is achieved (e.g., redundancy, predictive maintenance, rapid repair) and focuses on the measurable outcome in the problem domain. The phrase 'achieve and maintain' captures both the initial attainment and sustained performance without introducing implementation details.
refined_fr.verification_idea: Collect operational telemetry (uptime/downtime logs, scheduled vs. unscheduled maintenance events) over the agreed evaluation period. Calculate availability as (Total Time − Downtime) / Total Time. Compare against customer-defined target (e.g., ≥0.97). Verify that the measured availability meets or exceeds the threshold for the full evaluation window.
sub_frs: FR-NGHE-004.1: The NGHE solution shall achieve the customer-defined operational availability target at the start of the evaluation period.
FR-NGHE-004.2: The NGHE solution shall sustain operational availability at or above the customer-defined target throughout the agreed evaluation period.

candidate_dps: DP1: Implement redundant critical subsystems (power, control, sensing) with automatic failover logic to mask single-point failures and maintain service continuity.
DP2: Design modular architecture with hot-swappable components and standardized interfaces to enable rapid replacement of failed units without system shutdown.
DP3: Establish predictive maintenance framework using condition monitoring and degradation models to detect and address faults before they cause unavailability.
DP4: Implement graceful degradation strategy where non-critical functions are suspended to preserve core operational capability when faults occur.
DP5: Deploy distributed control with local autonomy and inter-node synchronization to eliminate central points of failure and enable partial operation.
DP6: Establish comprehensive fault detection and isolation (FDIA) system with automated diagnostics and operator alerting to minimize mean-time-to-repair (MTTR).
DP7: Combine redundancy (DP1), modularity (DP2), and monitoring (DP3) in an integrated availability architecture with defined recovery time objectives (RTO) and recovery point objectives (RPO).
selected_dp.selected_dp: DP7: Integrated availability architecture combining (1) redundancy of critical subsystems with automatic failover, (2) modular design with hot-swappable components and standardized interfaces, and (3) predictive condition monitoring with automated fault detection and isolation—all governed by explicit RTO/RPO targets aligned to the ≥0.97 availability threshold.

potential_sources: Redundancy and modularity are orthogonal concerns: redundancy addresses fault masking; modularity addresses repair speed. Both are necessary and independently valuable.
Monitoring (DP3) is loosely coupled to redundancy and modularity—it informs maintenance decisions but does not dictate failover or component design.

Risk: If RTO/RPO targets are not explicitly derived from the 0.97 availability requirement, the three pillars may be over- or under-specified. Requires quantitative availability modeling (e.g., Markov chains, fault tree analysis) to validate sufficiency.
Risk: Synchronization overhead in distributed control (if selected as IM) could introduce latency that violates RTO; must be validated against operational timing constraints.
coupling_check.notes: DP7 is a composite design that addresses availability through three independent mechanisms. Coupling is minimal because each mechanism targets a different failure mode: redundancy handles component failure, modularity handles repair logistics, and monitoring handles early detection. However, the integration point (RTO/RPO alignment) must be explicitly managed to avoid over-specification or gaps.

assumptions_risks: ASSUMPTION: Customer-defined target is ≥0.97 (97% availability) over the agreed evaluation period; if period is short (e.g., <30 days) or target is higher (e.g., 0.999), redundancy and monitoring complexity may increase significantly.
ASSUMPTION: 'Operational availability' includes both scheduled and unscheduled downtime; if only unscheduled downtime counts, maintenance windows must be planned outside evaluation period.

ASSUMPTION: Worksite safety plan and operational policies do not prohibit automated failover or remote diagnostics; if manual intervention is required for all state changes, MTTR will increase and availability target may be unachievable.
RISK: Redundancy cost and weight/space may be prohibitive in field deployment; DP7 assumes sufficient resources for dual or N+1 subsystems.

RISK: Predictive maintenance requires historical fault data and domain expertise to calibrate degradation models; if unavailable, condition monitoring may produce false positives/negatives, reducing effectiveness.
RISK: Distributed control introduces synchronization complexity; if inter-node communication is unreliable, local autonomy may cause inconsistent state and data loss.
RISK: RTO/RPO targets are not explicitly stated in the FR; if recovery time exceeds acceptable operational windows, availability target cannot be met regardless of DP quality.
RISK: Evaluation period duration not specified; short periods (e.g., <7 days) may not capture rare failure modes, leading to optimistic availability estimates.
selected_dp.recommended_revision: FR (refined): The NGHE solution shall achieve and maintain operational availability at or above the customer-defined target threshold (≥0.97 over the agreed evaluation period) by implementing an integrated availability architecture that combines (1) redundancy of critical subsystems with automatic failover (RTO ≤ [X] seconds), (2) modular design with hot-swappable components and standardized interfaces (MTTR ≤ [Y] minutes), and (3) predictive condition monitoring with automated fault detection and isolation (fault detection latency ≤ [Z] seconds). The solution shall operate within worksite safety and operational policy constraints and shall be validated through quantitative availability modeling (e.g., Markov analysis, fault tree analysis) to confirm that the combined effect of redundancy, modularity, and monitoring achieves the target threshold.

im_boundary.name: NGHE Solution
im_boundary.scope: Integrated availability architecture encompassing redundancy management, modular component design with hot-swap capability, predictive condition monitoring, fault detection/isolation logic, and RTO/RPO governance mechanisms across all subsystems

external_interfaces.name: Telemetry
external_interfaces.details: Ingests health metrics, component status, and condition data from Vehicle and Teleoperation subsystems to feed predictive monitoring and fault detection

external_interfaces.name: Tasking
external_interfaces.details: Directs failover commands, component isolation actions, and redundancy activation to Vehicle and Teleoperation subsystems

external_interfaces.name: SafetyAlert
external_interfaces.details: Broadcasts critical availability events, RTO/RPO threshold violations, and fault isolation decisions to Safety Services

external_interfaces.name: Network Link
external_interfaces.details: Maintains inter-subsystem communication for redundancy coordination, failover synchronization, and distributed health monitoring

module_containment_scores.notes: Highest containment: NGHE Solution is the system-level orchestrator responsible for integrated availability governance. Redundancy logic, failover automation, modular design principles, predictive monitoring, and RTO/RPO targets are all system-wide concerns. Minor diffusion: Vehicle and Teleoperation must implement standardized interfaces and respond to failover/isolation commands, but core availability architecture resides here.

Poor containment: Vehicle is a subsystem, not the system integrator. Redundancy coordination, predictive monitoring logic, and RTO/RPO governance span multiple subsystems (Vehicle + Teleoperation + Safety Services). Significant diffusion: failover logic, condition monitoring algorithms, and availability targets would scatter across Vehicle, Teleoperation, and Safety Services.

Poor containment: Teleoperation is a subsystem focused on remote operation, not system-level availability orchestration. Redundancy, failover, and RTO/RPO governance are cross-subsystem concerns. Significant diffusion: core availability logic would fragment across Vehicle, Teleoperation, and Safety Services, violating cohesion.

Poor containment: Safety Services is a specialized subsystem for safety-critical alerts and compliance, not the primary availability orchestrator. Redundancy management, failover automation, and predictive monitoring are broader system concerns. Significant diffusion: availability architecture would scatter across multiple subsystems; Safety Services would only handle alert dissemination, not core logic.

assigned_im.containment_hypothesis: DP7 (integrated availability architecture) is fundamentally a system-level design pattern that coordinates redundancy, failover, monitoring, and RTO/RPO governance across all subsystems. NGHE Solution, as the top-level system integrator, is the natural boundary for this DP. Vehicle, Teleoperation, and Safety Services are subsystems that implement standardized interfaces and respond to availability directives, but they do not own the orchestration logic. Containment is strong because all three pillars of DP7 (redundancy + modular design + predictive monitoring) require system-wide visibility and coordination that only the NGHE Solution level can provide.
containment_risks: Risk: Redundancy logic for Vehicle subsystem (e.g., dual motor failover) may be tightly coupled to Vehicle hardware; if Vehicle module is not designed with hot-swappable standardized interfaces, NGHE Solution cannot enforce modular design principle without Vehicle redesign.

Risk: Predictive condition monitoring requires real-time telemetry from all subsystems; if Telemetry interface is not sufficiently rich or latency-sensitive, NGHE Solution cannot achieve fault detection SLAs needed to meet RTO/RPO targets.

Risk: Failover automation (Tasking interface) must be deterministic and low-latency; if Network Link introduces unacceptable jitter or partitions, NGHE Solution's failover commands may not reach subsystems in time, violating RTO targets.

Risk: RTO/RPO governance is policy-driven; if Safety Services or other subsystems have conflicting availability policies, NGHE Solution must arbitrate—requires clear policy hierarchy and escalation paths.

Risk: Hot-swappable component design is a physical/architectural constraint; if Vehicle or Teleoperation subsystems are not designed with modular, standardized interfaces from the outset, retrofitting will be costly and may introduce new failure modes.
assigned_im.recommended_revision: DP7 is well-formulated and appropriately scoped to the system level. No revision needed. However, to strengthen containment and reduce diffusion risk, recommend explicit sub-DPs for each subsystem (Vehicle, Teleoperation, Safety Services) that detail how each subsystem implements standardized interfaces, responds to failover commands, and exposes health telemetry. This ensures that NGHE Solution's availability orchestration logic remains cohesive while subsystems are designed with availability-aware modularity from the start.

fr_dp_gate.reason: DP7 directly addresses the FR by specifying a coherent, multi-pillar approach (redundancy + modular design + predictive monitoring + RTO/RPO governance) to achieve and maintain ≥0.97 availability. The DP is solution-agnostic enough to accommodate different technologies while concrete enough to guide design decisions. Mapping is traceable: FR's 'achieve and maintain' → DP's 'integrated architecture' + 'explicit RTO/RPO targets'; FR's 'customer-defined target threshold' → DP's 'aligned to ≥0.97 availability threshold'.

diffusion_risks: Redundancy logic for Vehicle subsystem (e.g., dual motor failover, sensor redundancy) risks tight coupling to Vehicle hardware. If Vehicle module is not designed with hot-swappable standardized interfaces from inception, NGHE Solution cannot enforce modular design principle without costly Vehicle redesign. Mitigation: Require Vehicle IM to expose standardized redundancy interfaces (e.g., active/standby switchover API) as a sub-DP.

Predictive condition monitoring and fault detection algorithms may scatter across Vehicle, Teleoperation, and Safety Services if each subsystem implements its own monitoring logic. This violates cohesion and creates inconsistent fault detection SLAs. Mitigation: Centralize monitoring algorithms in NGHE Solution IM; subsystems expose telemetry via standardized Telemetry interface only.

Failover automation (Tasking interface) depends on deterministic, low-latency Network Link. If network partitions or introduces unacceptable jitter, NGHE Solution's failover commands may not reach subsystems within RTO window, violating availability targets. Mitigation: Define explicit Network Link SLAs (latency, partition tolerance) as a sub-DP constraint.

RTO/RPO governance is policy-driven. If Vehicle, Teleoperation, and Safety Services have conflicting availability policies (e.g., different failover priorities), NGHE Solution must arbitrate. Without clear policy hierarchy and escalation paths, governance logic will diffuse across subsystems. Mitigation: Define explicit policy arbitration rules in NGHE Solution IM; subsystems inherit policies, do not define them.

Hot-swappable component design is a physical/architectural constraint that must be enforced at subsystem design time. If Vehicle or Teleoperation are not designed with modular, standardized interfaces from the outset, retrofitting will be costly and introduce new failure modes. Mitigation: Require Vehicle and Teleoperation IMs to document standardized interface contracts (e.g., connector types, power/signal specs, hot-swap protocols) as sub-DPs.
dp_im_gate.containment_notes: NGHE Solution IM achieves strong containment (0.92 score) as the system-level availability orchestrator. Redundancy coordination, predictive monitoring logic, fault detection/isolation algorithms, and RTO/RPO governance are appropriately centralized here. Vehicle (0.45), Teleoperation (0.4), and Safety Services (0.35) are subsystems that implement standardized interfaces and respond to availability directives; they do not own orchestration logic. However, containment is conditional on subsystems being designed with availability-aware modularity (standardized interfaces, hot-swap capability, telemetry exposure) from inception. Minor diffusion remains: Vehicle and Teleoperation must implement failover response logic and expose health telemetry, but this is expected subsystem-level responsibility, not core availability architecture diffusion.

fr_im_gate.verification_feasibility: Verification of FR 'achieve and maintain ≥0.97 availability' is feasible via NGHE Solution IM. Acceptance criterion is quantitative and measurable: collect operational data over agreed evaluation period, compute availability = (total time - downtime) / total time, compare to ≥0.97 threshold. NGHE Solution IM must expose availability metrics (e.g., via telemetry or dashboards) and log all failover events, RTO/RPO violations, and fault isolation actions for post-evaluation audit. Subsystem IMs (Vehicle, Teleoperation, Safety Services) must provide raw health telemetry and respond to failover commands; NGHE Solution IM aggregates and reports system-level availability.
fr_im_gate.allocation_clarity: Allocation is clear: NGHE Solution IM owns integrated availability architecture (redundancy orchestration, predictive monitoring, fault detection/isolation, RTO/RPO governance). Vehicle IM owns redundancy implementation (e.g., dual motors, sensor fusion) and responds to failover commands via Tasking interface. Teleoperation IM owns remote operation redundancy (e.g., dual communication links) and responds to failover commands. Safety Services IM owns alert dissemination and compliance logging. Telemetry interface (in) and Tasking interface (out) are clear boundaries. Network Link (inout) is a shared infrastructure dependency that must be governed by explicit SLAs. Ownership candidate (NGHE Solution as System-Level Availability Orchestrator) is well-justified and reduces ambiguity.

recommended_revisions: Decompose DP7 into explicit sub-DPs for each subsystem: (1) Vehicle IM: DP7.1 'Redundant critical subsystems with standardized hot-swap interfaces and failover response logic'; (2) Teleoperation IM: DP7.2 'Redundant communication and control paths with automatic failover response'; (3) Safety Services IM: DP7.3 'Availability event alert dissemination and RTO/RPO violation logging'; (4) NGHE Solution IM: DP7.0 'System-level availability orchestration, predictive monitoring, and RTO/RPO governance'. This ensures subsystems are designed with availability-aware modularity from inception and reduces diffusion risk.

Define explicit Network Link SLAs as a sub-DP constraint: latency (e.g., <100ms for failover commands), partition tolerance (e.g., graceful degradation if link fails), and synchronization guarantees (e.g., eventual consistency for redundancy state). Verify that Network Link implementation (e.g., dual communication channels, redundant routers) meets these SLAs.

Specify standardized interface contracts for Telemetry (in), Tasking (out), and SafetyAlert (out) as sub-DPs: (1) Telemetry: define health metric schema, sampling frequency, and latency requirements; (2) Tasking: define failover command types, response timeouts, and acknowledgment protocols; (3) SafetyAlert: define alert severity levels, escalation rules, and logging requirements. This reduces ambiguity and enables subsystem teams to design independently.

Document RTO/RPO policy hierarchy and arbitration rules in NGHE Solution IM: (1) Define default RTO/RPO targets (e.g., RTO=5min, RPO=1min); (2) Allow customer to override targets per subsystem or component; (3) Define escalation path if conflicting policies arise (e.g., Safety Services requires immediate failover, Vehicle requires graceful shutdown). This prevents policy diffusion across subsystems.

Add acceptance criterion for modular design: 'All critical subsystems (Vehicle, Teleoperation) shall implement standardized hot-swap interfaces (e.g., connector types, power/signal specs, failover protocols) documented in design specifications. Retrofit cost and risk shall be assessed during design review.' This ensures DP7's modular design pillar is enforced at subsystem level.

Add acceptance criterion for predictive monitoring: 'NGHE Solution shall detect faults within [X] seconds of occurrence (e.g., 10s) and initiate failover within RTO window (e.g., 5min). Fault detection latency and failover response time shall be measured and logged for all evaluation period events.' This verifies that predictive monitoring and fault detection/isolation logic meet RTO targets.

decision.justification: All three gates (FR-DP, DP-IM, FR-IM) pass with high confidence. However, the gate results explicitly recommend decomposing DP7 into subsystem-specific sub-DPs and defining explicit sub-DP constraints (Network Link SLAs, standardized interface contracts, RTO/RPO policy hierarchy). The diffusion risks are significant and conditional on subsystem design choices made at the next decomposition level. Given high safety criticality, proceeding directly to STOP would leave critical architectural constraints (hot-swap interfaces, predictive monitoring centralization, policy arbitration) unverified at subsystem level. ZIGZAG_DOWN is appropriate: derive next-level sub-FRs that explicitly address subsystem-level availability modularity, interface standardization, and RTO/RPO governance, ensuring diffusion risks are mitigated before detailed design.
next_level_sub_frs: FR7.1: Implement redundant critical subsystems (Vehicle) with standardized hot-swap interfaces and automatic failover response capability, such that failed components can be replaced or switched without system shutdown and failover logic executes within RTO window.

FR7.2: Implement redundant communication and control paths (Teleoperation) with automatic failover response, such that loss of primary communication link triggers automatic switchover to secondary link within RTO window without manual intervention.

FR7.3: Implement system-level availability orchestration, predictive fault detection, and RTO/RPO governance in NGHE Solution, such that faults are detected within [X] seconds, failover commands are issued within RTO window, and policy conflicts are arbitrated deterministically.

FR7.4: Define and enforce explicit Network Link SLAs (latency, partition tolerance, synchronization guarantees) such that failover commands reach subsystems within RTO window and redundancy state remains consistent across subsystems.

FR7.5: Standardize health telemetry interface (Telemetry in) across all subsystems, such that NGHE Solution can aggregate metrics, detect faults, and compute system-level availability without subsystem-specific monitoring logic.

FR7.6: Standardize failover command interface (Tasking out) across all subsystems, such that NGHE Solution can issue deterministic failover directives and receive acknowledgments within defined response timeouts.

FR7.7: Define RTO/RPO policy hierarchy and arbitration rules in NGHE Solution, such that conflicting availability policies (e.g., Safety Services vs. Vehicle) are resolved deterministically and subsystems inherit policies rather than defining them independently.

revise.why: DP7 is logically sound but requires explicit sub-DP decomposition to address diffusion risks and subsystem-level modularity constraints. Recommended revisions from gate results should be incorporated: (1) DP7.0 'System-level availability orchestration, predictive monitoring, and RTO/RPO governance' (NGHE Solution IM); (2) DP7.1 'Redundant critical subsystems with standardized hot-swap interfaces and failover response logic' (Vehicle IM); (3) DP7.2 'Redundant communication and control paths with automatic failover response' (Teleoperation IM); (4) DP7.3 'Availability event alert dissemination and RTO/RPO violation logging' (Safety Services IM); (5) DP7.4 'Network Link SLAs: latency <100ms, partition tolerance, eventual consistency'; (6) DP7.5-7.7 'Standardized interface contracts for Telemetry, Tasking, SafetyAlert; RTO/RPO policy hierarchy and arbitration rules'. This ensures subsystems are designed with availability-aware modularity from inception and reduces diffusion risk.

assumptions: Vehicle IM can be redesigned or retrofitted with standardized hot-swap interfaces (e.g., active/standby switchover API) without prohibitive cost or schedule impact.
Teleoperation IM supports dual communication links and automatic failover response within RTO window.
Network Link infrastructure (e.g., dual communication channels, redundant routers) can meet explicit SLAs (latency <100ms, partition tolerance).
NGHE Solution IM can centralize predictive monitoring and fault detection algorithms without creating unacceptable latency or single-point-of-failure risk.
RTO/RPO policy hierarchy can be defined and enforced without requiring real-time consensus across subsystems.
Safety Services IM can log all availability events and RTO/RPO violations for post-evaluation audit without impacting system performance.
open_risks: Vehicle subsystem may not be designed with hot-swappable standardized interfaces from inception. Retrofitting could introduce new failure modes or exceed cost/schedule constraints. Mitigation: Require Vehicle IM design review to document standardized interface contracts and assess retrofit risk before detailed design.

Predictive monitoring algorithms may scatter across subsystems if NGHE Solution IM does not enforce centralized monitoring architecture. Mitigation: Define explicit Telemetry interface contract and require all subsystems to expose health metrics via this interface only; prohibit subsystem-specific monitoring logic.

Network Link SLAs may not be achievable with current infrastructure (e.g., latency >100ms, partition tolerance not supported). Mitigation: Conduct Network Link feasibility study and define achievable SLAs; adjust RTO targets if necessary.

RTO/RPO policy arbitration logic may become complex if subsystems have conflicting availability policies. Mitigation: Define clear policy hierarchy (e.g., Safety Services > Vehicle > Teleoperation) and escalation rules; document in NGHE Solution IM design specification.

Failover response latency may exceed RTO window if subsystems do not implement deterministic failover logic. Mitigation: Define failover response time acceptance criterion (e.g., <5 seconds) and measure during system integration testing.

High safety criticality requires rigorous verification of fault detection, isolation, and failover logic. Mitigation: Conduct FMEA on availability architecture; define fault injection test cases; require formal verification of critical failover paths.
evidence_needed: Vehicle IM design specification documenting standardized hot-swap interfaces (connector types, power/signal specs, failover protocols) and retrofit cost/risk assessment.
Teleoperation IM design specification documenting dual communication links, automatic failover response logic, and failover response time measurements.
Network Link feasibility study confirming achievable latency, partition tolerance, and synchronization guarantees; SLA documentation.
NGHE Solution IM design specification documenting predictive monitoring algorithms, fault detection/isolation logic, RTO/RPO governance, and policy arbitration rules.

Standardized interface contracts for Telemetry (health metric schema, sampling frequency, latency requirements), Tasking (failover command types, response timeouts, acknowledgment protocols), and SafetyAlert (severity levels, escalation rules, logging requirements).
RTO/RPO policy hierarchy and arbitration rules documented in NGHE Solution IM; customer approval of default RTO/RPO targets and override procedures.
Fault injection test plan and results demonstrating fault detection latency <[X] seconds and failover response time <RTO window.
FMEA on availability architecture identifying critical failure modes and mitigation strategies.
Operational data from evaluation period showing system-level availability ≥0.97, with detailed logs of failover events, RTO/RPO violations, and fault isolation actions.
